images.caption: Figure 1:Schematic representation of DrugGen-2 development and evaluation.The top section shows dataset preparation and the training process through supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and group relative policy optimization (GRPO) using a customized reward function. The bottom section outlines the assessment process, based on unique generation, validity, similarity to approved drugs, and binding affinity for DrugGen-2, DrugGen, and DrugGPT, along with docking simulations for DrugGen-2.

Figure 2:Evaluation of the DrugGen-2 algorithm.(A)Reward progression during group relative policy optimization (GRPO). Comparison of DrugGen-2 with DrugGen and DrugGPT in terms of(B)number of unique generations,(C)valid generations,(D)similarity to approved molecules, and(E)predicted binding affinity.(F)Visualization of protein–ligand complexes at the active site of 1UFZ (P12821), showing the binding of Enalapril (red), ligands P12821-293 (orange), P12821-10 (blue), P12821-269 (green), P12821-225 (yellow), and Captopril (pink).(G)Binding site of 4EMA (P37231) displaying Balsalazide (red), Rosiglitazone (pink), and ligands P37231-165 (orange), P37231-117 (blue), P37231-39 (green), and P37231-247 (yellow).(H)Visualization of 3D protein-ligand interactions, Rosiglitazone (top) and P37231-165 (bottom) in PPARγ\gamma’s active site.
